hook: You already know how to graph lines and parabolas using $y = mx + b$ and $y = x^2$, but those are just two members of a much richer family of curves hiding all around you — in satellite dishes, planetary orbits, and suspension bridges. Standard coordinates and equations only tell part of the story, leaving us without the tools to describe curves that are tilted, traced dynamically, or expressed in circular coordinates. What if one unified geometric idea could explain all of these shapes — and several powerful new ways to describe them?
concept: Every topic in this unit is a different lens for studying the *conic sections* — the curves formed by slicing a cone — and the coordinate systems that bring out their elegance. Together, these ideas form the foundation of *analytic geometry*: the marriage of algebra, geometry, and multiple coordinate frameworks.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to write and graph parabolas in standard conic form, identifying the focus and directrix to understand the reflective geometry that makes parabolas essential in optics and engineering.
Master the equation and graphing of ellipses, locating their foci and axes so you can describe everything from whispering galleries to the elliptical orbits of planets.
Apply the standard form of a hyperbola to graph its branches and asymptotes, building the skills needed to analyze GPS systems and other real-world hyperbolic models.
You will use rotation-of-axes formulas to eliminate the $xy$-term from a general second-degree equation, identifying and graphing conics that are tilted at an angle in the plane.

You will learn to plot points and graph curves using the polar coordinate system $(r, 	heta)$, discovering how circular grids make certain elegant curves — like rose curves and limaçons — beautifully simple to describe.
Master parametric equations by expressing $x$ and $y$ as separate functions of a third variable $t$, gaining the power to trace curves dynamically and model motion along a path.
You will unify the study of parabolas, ellipses, and hyperbolas by expressing all three conic sections through a single elegant polar equation, connecting eccentricity to the geometry of each curve.
